data.title: Moderna Vs BioNTech: Who's Winning The Post-COVID Race
data.description:
And so, I've reached the publication of the sixth article, following "Pfizer vs. Johnson & Johnson: Who's the Better Bargain for Investors" and "Eli Lilly vs. Novo Nordisk: Which Is the Better Bargain for Investors," in a series of comparison analyses of two pharmaceutical stocks.

In today's article, I'll choose which of BioNTech (NASDAQ:BNTX) and Moderna (NASDAQ:MRNA) is more attractive after the end of the COVID-19 era, whose pipeline of product candidates is more promising in the fight against cancer, and which of these two is more 'protected' from the attacks of President Trump and RFK Jr. on the vaccine industry.

So, let's get started.

Current Valuation of BioNTech vs. Moderna

As in my last comparison article, "Teva vs. Viatris: Who Will Dominate in President Trump's America," below you will see a table with the values of each of the parameters by which I will compare the two companies.

Further, I'll present a detailed analysis of those that, according to my "ALLKA 4D Rating" model, carry the most weight in determining whether BioNTech or Moderna is more undervalued within the "Current Valuation" category.

So, I'll start by comparing how Moderna and BioNTech's revenues changed after the COVID-19 pandemic ended and Robert F Kennedy Jr. took over as Secretary of HHS on February 13, 2025, an event that, in my view, led to a more restrictive vaccine policy against this virus.

Next, I'll determine how often these two stocks beat Wall Street analysts' expectations and only then compare their price-to-sales [P/S] ratios over the past 12 months.

Moderna's revenue was $142 million in Q2 2025, up 31.5% quarter-on-quarter but down 41.1% year-on-year.

Consequently, its P/S ratio [TTM] is 3.45x, 12.74% lower than the sector median and 85.6% lower than its 5-year average.

If I look at how many times in the last 12 quarters Moderna has beaten the consensus revenue estimate, the number is 10.

With BioNTech, the situation is a little different, it seems to me, for the obvious reason that its partner in the Comirnaty franchise, namely Pfizer (PFE), has more experience and a marketing budget to minimize the current situation.

What am I talking about?

By this 'situation', I mean that after the completion of the stimulus programs promoted by the White House from 2021 to 2023, people are becoming less eager to get vaccinated with the mRNA COVID-19 vaccines.

So, BioNTech's revenue was not only bigger than Moderna's, reaching about $301.6 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, but its growth rate was also higher, namely 113.9% year-on-year and 45.8% quarter-on-quarter.

Its P/S ratio [TTM] is 7.42x, which is 87.4% higher than the sector median.

Thus, I can only conclude that Moderna is significantly more undervalued by this metric than BioNTech.

How often has BioNTech beaten analysts' expectations?

It has beaten Wall Street's revenue estimates six times out of the last 12 quarters, four quarters fewer than Moderna.

Now I'll move on to discussing the balance sheets of both companies.

According to my "ALLKA 4D Rating" model, analyzing total debt, the total debt/EBITDA ratio, and the maturity dates of Moderna/BioNTech's notes is crucial, and I will explain why later.

Does BioNTech or Moderna have lower total debt and total debt/EBITDA ratio?

Moderna's total debt was $741 million at the end of Q2 2025, down $528 million over the past 12 months.

Meanwhile, its competitor's total debt in the vaccine market has fluctuated around $260.4 million over the past 10 quarters, reaching $317.6 million at the end of June.

Let's continue.

Since the operating income and EBITDA of Moderna and BioNTech are negative, I believe that instead of discussing their total debt/EBITDA ratios, it would be more appropriate to compare how much cash is on their balance sheets.

BioNTech's cash and short-term investments amount to about $16.5 billion, compared to Moderna's $5.1 billion.

Also, if I look at how it's declined year-on-year, BioNTech again emerges as the ‘winner’ [-$2 billion vs. -$3.36 billion in the case of Moderna].

Thus, I conclude that BioNTech has greater financial flexibility and, more importantly, its balance sheet allows it to be more aggressive in R&D.

I'm not even taking into account that it also has strong partnership agreements with Pfizer and, especially, Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) [up to $11.1 billion] .

I will discuss the deal between BioNTech and Bristol-Myers Squibb in the next section.

Since Moderna and BioNTech don't pay dividends, it's logical that I move on to the final question.

Who won in the "Current Valuation" category?

To answer this question, I created a heat map that shows the winner more clearly than a table.

As a result, BioNTech won in the "Current Valuation" category with 4 points, beating Moderna by 2 points.

Portfolio of drugs and product candidates of Moderna vs. BioNTech

In this category, my main goal is to determine whether Moderna or BioNTech has a better chance of catching up to the leaders in the vaccine and oncology treatment markets, such as Merck (MRK), Novartis (NVS), and Sanofi (SNY).

To choose the 'winner', I ask the first question.

Does BioNTech or Moderna have a more promising portfolio of approved vaccines?

Moderna's portfolio consists of Spikevax, mNEXSPIKE, an mRNA vaccine with one-fifth the dose of Spikevax, and mRESVIA, an RSV [respiratory syncytial virus] vaccine.

And it would seem that "more means better," but I believe it's not.

mRESVIA was first approved by the FDA on May 31, 2024, to protect adults over 60 years of age from lower respiratory tract illness caused by RSV, and its label was expanded on June 12, 2025, to also include those 18-59 years of age.

But when I opened Moderna's latest Form 10-Qs/10-K, I saw that its sales are not only negligible but almost zero.

I think the two key reasons for this current situation are people's distrust of mRNA vaccines after the 'COVID-19 era', and the fact that Pfizer's Abrysvo and GSK's Arexvy (GSK) remain the preferred choices of doctors.

Overall, sales of these two vaccines [$143 million vs. $88 million] are as follows.

Ultimately, I believe that weak sales of Moderna's three vaccines, as well as the lack of a partner like Pfizer that could share operating expenses, have hit its EBIT hard.

How hard?

Moderna's EBIT was -$907 million in Q2 2025, compared to -$514.4 million for BioNTech.

Who has a more promising portfolio of product candidates? Moderna or BioNTech?

In my opinion, the situation here is also ambiguous.

Moderna's portfolio is richer in experimental vaccines targeting a wide range of viruses, including HSV [mRNA-1608], Epstein-Barr virus [mRNA-1189 and mRNA-1195], and norovirus [mRNA-1403 and mRNA-1405].

But of course, its 'crown jewel' is mRNA-4157, an mRNA vaccine developed jointly with Merck to combat renal cell carcinoma, melanoma, bladder cancer, and non-small cell lung cancer.

While BioNTech is more focused on developing bispecific antibodies such as BNT327, BNT312 [in partnership with Genmab] (GMAB), and BNT314, as well as so-called ADCs [antibody-drug conjugates] against breast cancer and solid tumors.

So, I want to clarify here that BNT327 [BMS986545] is an anti-PD-L1/VEGF-A bispecific antibody.

On June 2, 2025, Bristol-Myers Squibb announced that it had entered into a deal with BioNTech to develop this product candidate, which, to my great surprise, is being developed for 20 indications as monotherapy and in combination with trastuzumab pamirtecan and BNT325.

BioNTech's R&D portfolio includes 50 projects, six of which are in Phase III. Moderna has 35 clinical-stage projects, seven of which are in pivotal clinical trials.

Who won in the "Approved Drugs and Pipeline Candidates" category?

Thus, BioNTech became the winner, scoring 7 points.

Technical analysis and Wall Street Ratings of Moderna stock vs. BioNTech stock

So, according to the parameter values that I have noted in the table below, Moderna is a more attractive stock if I take technical analysis into account only.

I want to start with a 1-M timeframe.

Moderna stock shows more signs of being 'oversold' than BioNTech stock.

So, the price of Moderna shares remains slightly above the first standard deviation band formed from the VWAP [-1σ], and its RSI [33.28] has been near the oversold zone since November 1, 2024.

While BioNTech shares remain near VWAP [$103.60], and according to my assessment, it is completing wave C as part of the "zigzag" corrective pattern. If I switch to the 1-W timeframe, I believe that its stock price may correct further until it reaches a strong support zone lying between $95-$95.70.

Does Moderna or BioNTech have a greater upside relative to the average price target?

Once again, I cannot do without the data provided by Seeking Alpha.

According to it, the average PT [price target] for BioNTech is $134.76, indicating a potential upside of about 31.73% from the current share price [$104.90]. Meanwhile, if I turn to Moderna, the PT for it is $44.30, implying an upside of 65.67%.

Who won in the "Technical Analysis and Wall Street Ratings" category?

Moderna won this category with 4 points, beating BioNTech by 3.5 points.

But I can't stop at technical analysis alone, so let's continue.

Long-term prospects of BioNTech vs. Moderna

What do I expect from Moderna in the long term?

I expect Moderna's non-GAAP earnings per share to grow to $3.20 in 2029.

This figure is slightly above the median of a broad range of Wall Street analyst expectations [from -$3.41 to $20.62] because I am more optimistic about mNEXSPIKE sales and also believe mRNA-4157 will be a 'blockbuster' in the treatment of melanoma.

What do I expect from BioNTech in the long term?

I estimate that BioNTech's non-GAAP EPS and revenue growth will be higher than Moderna's over the next five years, reaching $2.35 and $4.4 billion, respectively.

These figures are at the upper end of the analyst ranges [from -$7.75 to $3.42 and from $2.05 billion to $4.44 billion] due to my more optimistic expectations for Comirnaty sales, as well as the launches of BNT327 and trastuzumab pamirtecan.

Who won in the "Long-Term Prospects" category?

Moderna beat BioNTech by 7 points.

Takeaway

So, I've reached the final part of the article.

Who is the winner?

But before I answer this question, I want to note that I am starting to cover Moderna and BioNTech with a 'Buy' rating.

However, because BioNTech scored 0.7 points higher, according to my "ALLKA 4D Rating" model, it's a more promising stock than Moderna.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Did Pfizer Overpay For Yet Another Acquisition?
data.description:
Performance Assessment

Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) has underperformed the S&P500 since my last update on the stock:

Thesis

I like some of Pfizer's recent moves. However, it is not enough to make me a buyer of PFE stock just yet:

  1. Pfizer's purchase of Metsera seems to be done at a reasonable valuation.
  2. Pfizer has some relief from tariffs, but it comes at a hefty cost of pricing headwinds.
  3. PFE stock may look undervalued vs. peers, but a weak fundamentals outlook may justify that.
  4. The sellers look like they are losing speed, but there are still no signs of convincing buyers.

Pfizer's Purchase Of Metsera Seems To Be Done At A Reasonable Valuation

In my last article on Pfizer, I had anticipated that Pfizer may resort to M&A to get a foothold in the obesity management drug market following its failure of Danuglipron. I viewed such a move as risky due to Pfizer's track record of overpaying for M&A as it finds itself in a desperate situation to make up for loss of exclusivity (LOE) patent expirations for up to 40% of its revenue profile:

Discontinuation in the development of Pfizer's obesity management drug is a major setback, and substituting it with pricey M&A is a risk

- Hunting Alpha

Hence, it is no surprise that Pfizer is acquiring Metsera (MTSR), a clinical-stage biotechnology company (which means no commercial revenues yet) focused on obesity management drugs. The total transaction value for this deal that Pfizer is paying is around $7.6 billion. This includes an upfront value of $4.9 billion and contingent value rights worth around $2.7 billion associated with key drugs progressing through clinical trials. Here's an overview of the key drugs:

The main drug, MET-097i, is currently in Phase 2 and is expected to move on to Phase 3 trials.

Now, some pharmaceutical analysts have estimated peak sales potential of more than $5 billion for Metsera:

PFE’s planned acquisition of MTSR [Metsera] is set to add differentiated obesity drug candidates that we estimate could generate >$5B in combined peak sales potential

- Leerink Partners' Senior Managing Director and Senior Research Analyst David Risinger

But this annual revenue estimate is the best-case scenario. Ideally, this figure should be adjusted for the risks of failure during clinical trials. Here's how we can take that $5 billion number and arrive at a figure that is arguably more realistic:

This table below shows the chances of commercialization (called marketing authorization) based on various clinical trial stages. I highlighted the metabolism and endocrinology disciplines as obesity falls under these categories:

Assuming an equally weighted average of Phase II to commercialization success between the metabolism (25% chance) and endocrinology (15% chance) disciplines, we can get to an overall estimate of 20% success chance for Metsera's commercialization. But note how not all drugs are at Phase II. Hence, this 20% probability should be downgraded a bit. I'll assume 17%.

So applying 17% to $5 billion gets us $850 million for a risk-adjusted annual revenue estimate for Metsera. Now, we can also apply this 17% to the $2.7 billion mentioned earlier to get a risk-adjusted contingent value rights payout of $459 million. Add this to the $4.9 upfront consideration, and we get a risk-adjusted transaction consideration of about $5.4 billion.

Now, we have enough information to compute an implied EV/revenue transaction multiple; 5400/850 = 6.4x.

Now, for this kind of clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company involved in drug discovery at the $850 million revenue kind of scale, this implies a sectoral comparable transaction multiple of 8.5x:

As you can see, the Metsera deal's EV/revenues multiple of 6.4x is at a 25% discount to 8.5x. Of course, due to a 3-4 year time lag of these revenues kicking in, arguably this discount is actually narrower:

Subject to clinical and regulatory success, we anticipate Metsera's pipeline will deliver a series of launches beginning in the 2028-2029 time frame that would accelerate our growth trajectory

- Chief Strategy & Innovation Officer Andrew Simon Baum in the Metsera M&A call

But the broader point probably still applies; it looks like Pfizer has not grossly overpaid for Metsera after all. Hence their M&A entry into obesity management drugs may not be as risky as I had feared.

Pfizer Has Some Relief From Tariffs, But It Comes At A Hefty Cost Of Pricing Headwinds

It looks like in exchange for a 3-year exemption from tariff hits, Pfizer is not only committing $70 billion (amounting to 5.6 years of the TTM FCF run-rate of $12.4 billion), but also bucking to pressure from regulators to lower prices for various drugs due to programs such as the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), Medicare Drug Price Negotiation Program (MDPN) and the Most-Favored-Nation (MFN) pricing policies. A lot of these headwinds are expected to kick in over the next 3 years. This risks section in the latest 10-Q is well worth a careful read:

In the U.S., we expect to see continued focus by the U.S. government and states on regulating drug pricing and access to medicine, including but not limited to, the potential for international reference pricing, including Most-Favored-Nation (MFN) drug pricing…

Eliqis was among the first ten medicines subject to the Medicare Drug Price Negotiation Program. In August 2024, the government released the new Medicare price for Eliquis, which, effective January 1, 2026, will be required to be offered to all Medicare beneficiaries and to covered entities participating in the 340B Program that dispense Eliquis to a Medicare beneficiary if that maximum fair price is lower than the discounted price such entities are offered under the 340B Program ceiling price calculation. The Eliquis Medicare price is factored into our long-term financial planning, in accordance with our standard financial reporting and forecasting protocols. On January 17, 2025, CMS announced the selection of another 15 drugs from Medicare Part D for the maximum fair price, with prices to be set and effective on January 1, 2027. Ibrance and Xtandi were included in the list of 15 drugs selected. Another 15 drugs from Medicare Part B or Medicare Part D will be selected by February 1, 2026, for the maximum price to be set and in effect by January 1, 2028. It is possible that more of our products could be selected in future years, which could, among other things, lead to lower revenues prior to expiry of intellectual property protections… The IRA also made significant changes to the Medicare Part D benefit design (IRA Medicare Part D Redesign), which took effect beginning in 2025 and are impacting our revenues. In 2025, we expect a favorable impact from the $2,000 annual out-of-pocket cap and new Prescription Payment Plan, more than offset by an expected unfavorable impact from the sunsetting of the Coverage Gap Discount Program and the addition of new manufacturer discounts in the initial and catastrophic coverage phases, which we anticipate will have a net unfavorable impact to revenue in 2025 of approximately $1 billion, year-over-year.

- Q2 FY25 10Q, Author's bolded highlights

Note that via the direct-to-consumer website TrumpRx, Pfizer is also taking pricing cuts on 4 prescription drugs to the tune of 40-85%.

Overall, this is likely to pressure gross profit margins ahead:

Now some bulls may point out that the benefit of $7.2 billion in net cost savings, which amounts to an 11.3% point boost to the margin profile:

On Track to Deliver Approximately $7.2 Billion in Overall Anticipated Net Cost Savings from Previously Announced Cost Improvement Initiatives(4) by End of 2027

- Q2 FY25 Press Release

But I would counter this point by drawing your attention to the 'unusual' charges that tend to be rather recurring. It's not unusual by any means:

Overall, these recurring annual charges have led to an average 12 percentage point margin hit. I argue that this would offset a lot of the margin benefits from cost saving efforts:

PFE Stock May Look Undervalued Vs. Peers, But A Weak Fundamentals Outlook May Justify That

PFE is trading at a 1-yr FWD P/E of 8.6x; a sizable 41% discount to the pharma peers' median multiple:

Note that this discount is steeper than the typical 22.6% discount that PFE has usually traded at vs. its peers:

So based on this, one may argue that PFE is relatively undervalued. I accept that maybe it is. But on the other hand, I wonder if the steeper discount is justified given the weaker fundamentals as seen in the downward to flattish trend in the earnings expectations index:

The Sellers Look Like They Are Losing Speed But There Are Still No Signs Of Convincing Buyers

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return, as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of PFE vs SPX500

On PFE vs. the S&P500 on the monthly charts, we see a continuation of a long-time underperformance downtrend since 2023. A point in favor of the bulls is that the momentum of the sellers is reducing. However, I would still insist that this is not a buy yet because there are no signs of convincing buyer sponsorship. Buying too early without these signs can lead to greater chances of dead money (low IRRs) tied up in this stock.

Takeaway & Positioning

After Pfizer failed to crack the obesity management market, I expected the company to resort to M&A to enter this market. I viewed this as a risk because I have noted that Pfizer tends to overpay for acquisitions, especially in the current arguably desperate context of needing to make up for lost revenues arising from patent expirations. But my analysis of the Metsera acquisition reveals that Pfizer may be accomplishing an M&A-based entry into the obesity drug market at a rather reasonable valuation.

On the costs and profitability side, I have concerns of pricing headwinds and 'unusual' charges that are far too frequent and recurring to be treated as one-off hits to the P&L. This paints a weak fundamental earnings outlook, which may justify PFE stock's valuation discount vs its other pharmaceutical peers.

And technically, vs. the broader market index, PFE's sellers look like they may be slowing down. However, I do not see any signs of strong buyers yet to mark a reversal of the overall downtrend.

Rating: 'Market Perform/Hold'

How To Interpret Hunting Alpha's Ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence.

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: In Search Of Stability: 4 Top Consumer Staples Stocks
data.description:
Trade War-Fueled Volatility: From Greed to Fear

The market has swung wildly in a tug-of-war between sentiments of fear and greed, marked by the Cboe Volatility Index (VIX) spiking twice in seven days. AI-fueled enthusiasm led to record highs before the market was dragged down by rising trade tensions, a government shutdown, and anxiety over the possibility of another steep correction. As the US and China renewed their trade war, ignited by Beijing’s rare earth export restrictions, the S&P 500 saw its worst sell-off since the “Tariff Tantrum” correction in April.

Investors continued to seek shelter in safe haven assets, with gold extending its record rally and silver prices reaching their highest in decades. Traders also flocked to the safety of bonds, with the benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) hitting its lowest level in seven months.

The surging volatility comes as high valuations are bringing back memories of the dotcom era’s irrational exuberance. At the end of Q2 2025, the S&P 500 P/E ratio stood at 28.3x, above five-year and 10-year averages. JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon told the BBC he was more worried than others of a serious market downturn, driven by uncertainty, geopolitical risks, and fiscal spending. IMF chief Kristalina Georgieva observed global equity prices are surging on AI optimism - but so is global uncertainty. And while the global economy has proven resilient, overcoming the impact of tariffs, Georgieva expressed concerns over the possibility of a sudden market downturn:

"History tells us this sentiment can turn abruptly. Today’s valuations are heading toward levels we saw during the bullishness about the internet 25 years ago. If a sharp correction were to occur, tighter financial conditions could drag down world growth, expose vulnerabilities, and make life especially tough for developing countries."

At times of rising uncertainty and instability, stocks in defensive sectors - like consumer staples, utilities, and healthcare - have served as shelter from the storm. At the end of last week, investors temporarily turned to these defensive stocks as the S&P 500 slid, before the index bounced back when tensions eased and the Fed signaled it may halt quantitative tightening. So far this year, S&P 500 consumer staples stocks have been among the worst performing as investors pile into growth and cyclical plays amid AI hysteria and interest rate cuts.

S&P 500 Performance by Sector (as of 10/17/2025)

Amid stretched valuations in growth sectors and mounting macro uncertainty, investors may find it worthwhile to revisit the stability offered by staples. Due to their non-cyclical nature, consumer staple stocks were the highest performers during economic slowdowns and recessions from 1960-2019. Consumer staples can be characterized as needs vs. wants – they include essential products and services that consumers will continue to buy regardless of economic conditions.

Staples are also low-volatility stocks. The S&P 500 Consumer Staples Select Sector ETF (XLP) has a 60M beta of 0.54 and a 25M beta of 0.28 – indicating they're less volatile than any other sector. In addition, staples are trading at attractive valuations, presenting buy-the-dip opportunities along with positioning for the next sector rotation. But not all staples stocks have underperformed.

Top Consumer Staples Stocks For Stability

Seeking Alpha Quant identified four consumer staples stocks spanning the food and tobacco industries, showcasing strong collective investment fundamentals, excellent momentum, high growth, and attractive valuations. Defying the bearish trend in the staples sector, all four stocks are outperforming the market in the past year.

1. United Natural Foods, Inc. (UNFI)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.55B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Food Distributors

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/17/25): 2 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/17/25): 1 out of 7

United Natural Foods is the No. 1 quant-rated food distributor stock, which has crushed the sector and market in the past year, driving an A+ Momentum Grade. A wholesaler with major clients like Whole Foods, UNFI believes it's well positioned to drive profitable growth within a growing $90B target addressable market that includes natural, organic, specialty, multicultural, and conventional grocery retailers, who can benefit from the company’s differentiated products, programs, and services. UNFI is executing on a refresh strategy and multi-year plan unveiled a year ago, focused on enhancing efficiencies and creating more value for customers and suppliers by leveraging automation, technology, and network optimization.

UNFI 1Y Price Performance vs. S&P 500

UNFI surged after beating Q4 and full-year earnings and expectations and boosting guidance, overcoming a $400M loss in sales from a cyberattack in June. UNFI annual net sales rose 2.6% to $31.8B in FY25, driven by 9% growth in the company’s Natural segment, larger volumes, and improved product mix. Adjusted EBITDA was up 6.6% to $518M for the fiscal year. UNFI has delivered robust growth in operating profit in the past 12 months, while levered free cash flow surged over 1,000%, boosted by network optimization and the consolidation of volumes from four distribution centers. UNFI deployed lean daily management in 28 of 52 distribution centers, which has strengthened performance across quality, delivery, and cost metrics.

Wall Street sell-side analysts have high-growth expectations, with the forward EPS long-term growth rate at 138%. UNFI has nine upward earnings revisions in the past 90 days, and EPS is expected to surge more than 180% in FY25, according to consensus estimates, and more than 20% in each of the following two years. The outstanding performance and high growth expectations have driven an A+ Growth Grade, and despite this hot momentum, the stock still trades at a value. UNFI’s PEG FWD of 0.15 represents a 94% discount to the sector for an A+ Valuation Grade. UNFI’s exceptional momentum, ability to overcome challenges to deliver growth, and attractive price make it a top Strong Buy in the staples sector.

2. Tesco PLC (OTCPK:TSCDY)

  • Market Capitalization: $38.52B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Food Retail

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/17/25): 3 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/17/25): 1 out of 17

The No. 1 quant-rated food retail stock, grocery giant Tesco has a solid position in its two biggest markets with a 28% share in the UK and 23% in Ireland. Tesco’s market share has gradually expanded over the past three years due to brand perception, price competitiveness, and improved quality and service. Tesco has handily outperformed the market and the sector in price performance over the past 12 months and in total return over the past five years.

In H125, Tesco revenue grew 5.1% to £33.05B and adjusted operating profit by 1.6% to £1.67B. Tesco Finest premium brand grew sales by 16%, while online revenue rose 11% to £3.7B. Tesco launched 470 new products and opened 38 stores in the first half of 2025, including 32 in the United Kingdom, three in Ireland, and three in Central Europe. Tesco's competitive pricing programs – Aldi Price Match, Low Everyday Prices, and Clubcard – were key drivers of volume growth and market share gains. The extension of internal cost reduction and efficiency initiatives offset operating cost inflation, including increased regulatory costs, to maintain margins. Tesco raised full-year operating profit guidance to between £2.9B and £3.1B, citing better-than-expected customer responses to pricing programs, cost control, and good weather.

Tesco’s EPS long-term forward growth rate is at 10.55%, while EPS is expected to grow by 6.9% in FY26 and 10.29% in FY27. Tesco’s P/E is around the sector median, but the overall valuation framework is appealing based on attractive PEG FWD and EV/EBITDA multiples. Tesco offers a dividend yielding 3.22%, with a 4.29% five-year growth rate, awarded semiannually. Tesco has an expanding foothold in the grocery retail market in the United Kingdom and Ireland, building momentum and potentially another food industry stock to add to your portfolio that provides geographic diversification.

3. Imperial Brands PLC (OTCQX:IMBBY)

  • Market Capitalization: $31.95B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Tobacco

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/17/25): 4 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/17/25): 1 out of 9

The top quant-rated tobacco stock, Imperial Brands registered its best single-session gain of the year earlier in October, after announcing a £1.45B share buyback plan and confirming it was on track to achieve annual targets for FY25 (ending on Sept. 30). According to Morgan Stanley, the update from Imperial Brands will be well received on Wall Street considering broader weakness elsewhere in staples. Imperial Brands and large-cap peers Philip Morris International Inc. (PM), British American Tobacco p.l.c. (BTI) and Altria Group, Inc. (MO), have shown excellent momentum in the past 12 months, defying the sector trend.

Imperial Brands 1Y Price Return vs. Industry Peers

The tobacco giant is committed to gaining market share in next generation products (NGP) - such as vapes, heated tobacco, and nicotine gum - which amounted to only £160M in adjusted net revenue in H125. Imperial Brands is aiming to grow NGP by double digits in the next five years. NGP net revenue in H125 grew 15% and is expected to come in at +12-14% for the fiscal year. Net revenue growth in both segments in the second half of the year was underpinned by strong pricing – offsetting lower volumes. Imperial expects consolidated net revenue growth in the low single digits and adjusted EPS growth to be in the high single digits. IMBBY's robust EBITDA, net income, and levered free cash flow margins are driving an ‘A’ Profitability Grade.

Imperial Brands is trading at a mere 9x earnings vs. the sector’s 15x, and a solid dividend yield is supporting a B+ Valuation Grade. Imperial Brands has a dividend yielding 6.2% with excellent growth and 27 consecutive years of payments. IMBBY’s poor Dividend Safety Grade is the result of high leverage, although the company has solid cash payout and coverage ratios and is expecting adjusted net debt to EBITDA at the lower end of 2.0–2.5x for FY25. data.title: Post Holdings: Moving On Without Some Of 8th Avenue's Assets
data.description:
In June, I concluded that Post Holdings (NYSE:POST) was adding another interesting chapter to its M&A strategy. The company has a distinctive capital allocation strategy, including a focus on share repurchase over dividends, with a higher tolerance for leverage.

Following a substantial and apparently appealing acquisition over the summer, the company is now divesting some of these recently acquired assets, at what appear to be not too inspiring multiples, making me a bit more cautious here. This comes on top of management's tolerance for leverage being higher than my own.

Diversified Consumer Brands

Post Holdings was spun-off from Ralcorp back in 2012, to become a diversified food business. Over the past decade, the company has grown sales by more than three billion to $8 billion, with these revenues generated from some four segments.

Post Consumer Brands makes up nearly half of sales, which is comprised of consumer brands and pet brands, with products offered including peanut butter, pet foods, and others, with brands in the portfolio including the likes of Malt-O-Meal, Peter Pan, Nature's Recipe, Gravy Train, etc.

Foodservice makes up a third of sales, largely consisting of its eggs business. These key segments are complemented by smaller refrigerated retail and Weetabix businesses.

Compared to other consumer packaged businesses, the company focuses on high leverage, but has few commitments to investors, as it does not pay out dividends, but is consistently a buyer of its own stock. The lack of dividends paid has been helpful to drive nominal capital gains, as shares have tripled over the past decade, translating into solid returns to investors.

Picking Up The Valuation

In November of last year, the company reported a 13% increase in fiscal 2024 sales, with revenues up to $7.9 billion, thanks to the purchase of pet food assets during the year. Adjusted earnings came in at $6.27 per share, and despite the prevalence of 13 adjustments to earnings, the gap with GAAP earnings at $5.64 per share was rather modest.

Net debt of $6.0 billion was substantial, for leverage ratios in the low 4s, with EBITDA trending around $1.4 billion. The company guided for 2025 EBITDA to advance in a minor way to $1.41-$1.46 billion.

Trading at $109 in the summer, shares of Post were valued at a $6.8 billion equity valuation, for a $13 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at about 1.6 times sales and 9 times EBITDA.

While leverage was substantial at $6.3 billion as the summer, Post felt comfortable increasing net debt further, following the announcement of a $880 million deal to acquire 8th Avenue Food & Provisions. This transaction adds $115 million in EBITDA on $1.1 billion in sales generated from dry pastas, nuts, granola, and fruit & nut products.

Valued at 7.6 times EBITDA ahead of anticipated synergies of some $15 million , pro forma EBITDA was seen moving up a long way to the $1.6 billion mark, needed with pro forma net debt seen around $7.2 billion.

With shares trading at 17-18 times adjusted earnings, I believed that valuations were fair, although I did not like the high leverage, seen over 4 times. However, Post has built up a solid track record, including the usage of leverage, as capital gains seen in the stock far outpace that of peers in recent years (which, in all fairness, do typically pay handsome dividends).

Coming Down A Bit

Since the summer, shares of Post have lost a few dollars, now trading down to the $106 mark. This followed some more moving targets over the summer, including the release of third quarter results in August.

Reported sales rose by 2% to $1.98 billion as the company hiked the full year midpoint of the EBITDA guidance to $1.51 billion, reflective of the contribution of 8th Avenue in the fourth quarter. Net debt was reported as pretty flattish at $6.3 billion, yet this is ahead of the closing of the deal for 8th Avenue, with the share count standing at 62 million shares on a diluted basis, this including potential divergence from convertible notes outstanding, with the common share count standing at 55 million shares.

Later that month, the company announced the sale of the pasta business on 8th Avenue in a $375 million cash deal, while assuming $80 million in leaseback liabilities. This came after the company closed on the deal for 8th Avenue on the 1st of July, so on the first day of the fourth quarter.

The company indicates that the remaining part (which will be held onto) of the 8th Avenue business will contribute $45-$50 million in EBITDA in 2026, which, in my view, suggests that the divested assets generate some $65-$70 million in EBITDA.

Reading the press release carefully, it looks like the company is divesting a lot of EBITDA at relatively modest multiples in this deal. With pro forma leverage seen up to $7.2 billion upon the deal for 8th Avenue, pro forma net debt is seen around $6.8 billion if we account for this divestment. However, as always, money is burning a hole in the pocket of management, with a new half-a-billion buyback program being announced.

What Now?

Year-to-date adjusted earnings are up 41 cents to $5.13 per share, making for realistic earnings between $6.50 and $7.00 per share, roughly translating into a 15-16 times earnings multiple. This comes as the business has been taking on a bit more leverage, which makes investors a bit more cautious, as I have some questions on the recent M&A moves.

My caution, despite reasonable earnings multiples and a solid track record, is based on the observation that many consumer packaged foods companies typically command lower multiples here. In all fairness, these peers have less of a track record as well, as the strategy of Post to employ aggressive leverage really does not fit my style.

In between all this, I am urging for a bit more conservative stance of the business, and while shares have seen a modest pullback from the highs, I am not enticed to buy the dip just yet, although I am keen to follow Post's next M&A move from here onwards.

data.title: U.S.-China Rare Earth Feud Jolts Markets, Oct. 24 May Decide The Trend
data.description:
The CCP's 4th Plenum is almost here

Investors in the market (NYSEARCA:SPY)(NASDAQ:QQQ)(NYSEARCA:IVV)(NYSEARCA:VOO)(NYSEARCA:DIA) have been on edge the past few days at the prospect of a new trade war brewing. This time the first shot seems to have been levied by China, in terms of registration requirements in China regarding what the end use of rare earth metals would be used for. The biggest implications here are that Chinese rare earth exports might be restricted for foreign military usage:

Beijing will not allow the export of rare earth materials for use by foreign militaries, China’s Ministry of Commerce announced on Oct. 9. These are the first restrictions imposed by China that specifically target the defense sector, according to Gracelin Baskaran, a critical minerals expert at the Center for Strategic and International Studies.

“What this essentially means is that it will deny licenses to foreign militaries and companies that are producing military use end goods,” Baskaran told CNBC. “It undermines the development of the defense industrial base at a time when there is rising global tension. It is a very powerful negotiating tactic because it undermines national security.”

This has sent critical and rare earth mineral companies soaring at the prospects that the US will need to shore up it's own rare earth supply for its military. Counter measures by the Trump administration are possibly adding an additional 100% tariffs to Chinese imports:

President Trump said Friday he will impose an additional 100% tariff on imports from China starting next month, citing new Chinese export controls — marking a significant escalation in the U.S.-China trade war.

The new 100% tariffs will add on to any existing import taxes on Chinese goods, the president wrote in a Truth Social post. Tariffs on Chinese imports currently stand at 30%. Mr. Trump said the U.S. will also impose export controls on "any and all critical software" starting next month.

The tariffs could be imposed on Nov. 1 or sooner, "depending on any further actions or changes taken by China," Mr. Trump said.

The US market might have misjudged this a bit and the Chinese announcement could possibly be about their own politics rather than foreign matters. With the 4th plenum upcoming, the timing is curious.

What is the 4th Plenum?

The plenum is a gathering of the CCP politburo [24 members, reduced from 25 in 2022] and Central Committee [370 members], this is the 4th meeting of the 5 year meeting cycle for the central committee which was elected in 2022. During the prior 4th plenums, this was historically the meeting where party governance and leadership is discussed. This includes leadership over the military and possible succession plans if any opposing members want to discuss the matter.

From October 20 to 23, the Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) will convene in Beijing for its annual plenum, the most important event in China’s political calendar this year. This meeting will focus on outlining the CCP’s next five-year plan, which the National People’s Congress—China’s rubber-stamp legislature—will approve in March 2026. The plenum may also see important personnel moves.

This [the 4th plenum] is known to be a pivotal meeting. Although the west sees China as a closed box of self election with little to no democratic involvement, the party has several factions that are not all aligned. Here is a brief breakdown:

The makeup of the politburo

Faction / Group Base / Core Identity
Xi Jinping’s Faction “Zhejiang Clique” / “Xi Loyalists” — Fujian, Zhejiang
Shanghai Clique Jiang Zemin allies
Tuanpai (Youth League Faction) Hu Jintao / Li Keqiang network, officials from Communist Youth League
Princelings Children of revolutionary leaders
PLA / Military-Aligned Generals and defense officials
Retired Elders / Party Seniors Former Politburo members

As we can see, all of these party members come from different regions of China that have governed different regional municipalities over time. The historical inner workings are not as important as to understand that Chinese typically have business interests locally to where they have their comeuppance or to where they are born. Xi, spent the majority of his career in the south. The largest rare earth interest are in the North, in and around Inner Mongolia. There is a good chance that factions within the politburo outside of Xi's loyalists have interests in rare earth mining and exports.

Although the majority of these are "state owned enterprises", there are still regional leaders that have business interests in SOEs. Here is a list of some of the largest mines:

Trump's Nov 1st deadline is not arbitrary

The deadline to come to an agreement on rare earths with China was moved to November 1st, just after the end of the 4th plenum. The US may be aware of internal Chinese politics and be giving them space to settle their own disputes. If this is just a power move ahead of the plenum for securing control by Xi, then this date would make sense as all of the decisions that would require some influential leverage would be over by that time [Nov 1st]. If restrictions are not negotiated by that time, then this may actually be a new CCP stance that Trump needs to deal with accordingly.

Who can the US turn to ?

Source: Melissa Pistilli of INN:

Rank Country Approx. Production in 2024 (metric tons) Simple Descriptor
1 China - 270,000 Dominant producer by far
2 United States -45,000 Top non-China producer
3 Myanmar - 31,000 Significant emerging source
4 Australia - 13,000 Major producer in Oceania
5 Nigeria -13,000 Leading African producer
6 Thailand - 13,000 Rapidly increasing output
7 India - 2,900 Modest but significant share
8 Russia - 2,500 Stable contributor
9 Madagascar - 2,000 Island source in Africa
10 Vietnam - 300 Smaller but in top 10

The above conflicts a bit with China's own accounting of rare earth reserves:

Regardless of the truth of who comes in what place after China, the truth is none have the supply chain and refinement capacity that China has set up regardless of their reserves. The US could continue to invest in early stage mineral producers, or they could try to help those with real capital pivot.

The energy, critical mineral and rare earth names if things turn south

As for the upstarts, there has been some major run ups for rare earth, nuclear and domestic lithium producers. Many of these have been on absolute fire this year but remain small to mid cap and might be worth a diversified investment knowing that the world is in a commodity hoarding phase. MP Materials (MP), when it comes to actual rare earth mining, is the lone horse in this race of small caps. They will need lots of funding and regulatory line cutting to get their project up to speed to where the defense sector would need them [if military exports of rare earth minerals are blocked].

My personal favorite choices

Rather than put money on these under funded small and mid caps that are probably 5-10 years out from being able to generate any real production, why not go with some proven domestic and friendly international metals mining companies that could pivot their operations should the demand curve meet their return requirements to make a pivot. Rio Tinto (RIO) BHP Group (BHP) Vale SA (VALE) and Southern Copper Corp. (SCCO) are all profitable, well capitalized and some have expressed interest in exploring rare earths as part of their mining outside of copper, nickel and other ores. The tailings alone may have significant rare earth within them if market demand makes it worthwhile to extract.

As we can see, none of these are even at a positive gain this year. Mining is difficult and capital intensive. Does the market really believe that MP materials or Lithium Americas will be able to both hire American workers and refine their minerals for a margin to where raw goods users would need them so they themselves can turn a profit? It would need a lot of government equity injections and still probably need a partnership with one of these large cap metals miners in order to really move dirt at the same speed as China.

On top of that, rather than being at all time highs, only Southern Copper is at their all time high, the rest are a long ways off.

Here are some 2 examples of these big 4 mentioning rare earth elements as a possible part of their future business:

Rio Tinto

May 1st 2025:

Rio Tinto is weighing a move into rare earths and other critical minerals as it responds to shifting global market dynamics and trade tensions.

Following the company’s annual general meeting in Perth on Thursday, chief executive Jakob Stausholm said the board had discussed rare earths this week and would take a “serious look” at their potential role in Rio Tinto’s portfolio.

Stausholm said that as the company continues to optimize its iron ore operations in the Pilbara and advances developments like the Simandou iron ore project in Guinea, it’s also reshaping its aluminum, copper, and lithium businesses to support the energy transition.

“So you could say, the next thing is to look a little bit deeper on critical minerals, and you have to think about that, not necessarily as separate mines,” Stausholm told reporters. He noted critical minerals are often present in Rio’s existing operations as a by-product, so “it’s a question of whether we should process them more deliberately.”

BHP

From 2023:

Rare Earth Elements • BHP’s Olympic Dam is a globally significant REE deposit (second largest concentration of REE globally). However, the low grade of REE makes it a challenge to bring to market. • Government investment in improving the ability to process low-concentration resources could provide a potential path for additional REE concentrate produced in Australia where low concentration is common amongst deposits. In addition, any support must acknowledge that the complexity of REE concentrate processing requires the need for significant chemical expertise and solutions to manage the resulting dangerous waste products that occur.

I want to clarify, it's not that these companies have solid plans to pursue rare earth markets, it's that they are at least as likely if not more likely to be able to pivot into these market as the small, zero profit and little revenue small caps have of producing significant amounts of rare earth minerals for the market. I believe these two outcomes are equally as likely and it's much safer to go with well seasoned companied that have not even appreciated substantially this year.

The end of the month should tell us much more

The question is not whether the US needs these elements now or later, the question is how fast do they need to develop the domestic and friendly foreign supply chain. Regardless of a big Chinese re-negotiation coming after the 4th plenum, the US is not going to want to be held hostage by these commodities, just like they didn't want to be by middle eastern oil. The US now is now one of the largest oil exporters in the world and it could be assumed that a new wick has been lit to set up an entirely new rare earth supply chain in the future.

Summary

I do think the timing of the Trump administration to delay tariffs till November 1st is indicative of the team knowing they need to back off China until they get through this big meeting. I personally don't think this is a moment where rare earth is completely choked off from China, nor do I believe China could 100% control their own supply chain just as the US has a hard time controlling the buying and selling of oil from adversaries and where US engineered GPUs end up. China is much larger and might be one of these most difficult countries to regulate in terms of commerce from the inside. That being said, now is a good time to contemplate where the supply chain will be developed and with whom.

data.title: XLU: Utilities Poised To Offer Blistering Returns, Strong Buy
data.description:
The utilities sector market dynamics shifted significantly in the past year as the robust demand from power-hungry data centers began to force power companies to invest hundreds of billions of dollars to enhance power generation. The bullish conditions also lifted electric utility companies expectations to earn a high single- to double-digit earnings growth over the long term.

Therefore, I believe it is the best time to initiate a stake in utilities sector to capitalize on the potential returns. Therefore, I upgrade my rating on The Utilities Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLU) to a strong buy from buy.

Data Centers And Power Consumption

After a decade of sluggish US electricity consumption, the market trends indicate a massive and prolonged rebound is ahead, which could significantly surpass the supply. While the electric cars and increasing industrial footprints are adding to demand, the power-hungry AI data centers are likely to contribute substantially to electricity demand.

Currently, the data centers account for nearly 9% of US electric consumption. According to the EIA, the data center's power consumption is expected to represent almost half of the demand growth in the next five years. In fact, the trend is expected to bolster over the long term. According to the National Electrical Manufacturers Association, the data centers energy consumption could surge by 300% in the next 10 years.

Utilities Earnings Growth Outlook Appears Promising

The sector's earnings growth forecast, a key factor for price and dividend returns, seems strong.

FactSet data shows that the utilities sector is expected to report 17% earnings growth in the third quarter and 11% in the final quarter of 2025. Moreover, the data also indicates a double digit percentage growth in the next year.

Companies in the utilities sector have also been presenting a promising earnings growth outlook with the massive investment plans to improve their power generation.

For example, NextEra Energy (NEE), the largest company in the utilities sector, reported 9% year over year earnings growth in the latest quarter.

The company stated that it expects to deliver adjusted earnings per share near the top end of its guidance of $3.63 to $4.00 and $3.85 to $4.32 in fiscal 2026 and 2027, respectively. Moreover, the company also anticipates to lift its dividends by 10% over the long term. Its share price is up 18% in the last six months.

Constellation Energy Corp's (CEG) share price rallied 77% year to date as it presented an aggressive investment plan to capitalize on power demand. It also announced a long term power purchase agreement with Meta for over 1,100 megawatts. On the other hand, Constellation expects its earnings to increase around 13% through the decade.

Duke Energy (DUK) expects to deliver 5% to 7% earnings growth over the next five years. Vistra Corp's share price soared more than 60% in the last twelve months as it aims to achieve a record adjusted EBITDA of $5.5 billion to $6.1 billion.

Vistra (VST) plans to acquire 7 natural gas facilities to improve its capacity by 2,600 megawatts. The company's share price soared 52% year to date. Other companies also seek to capitalize on growth trends while returning significant share price and dividend returns.

XLU Could be a Solid Vehicle to Ride the Long-Lasting Bull Run

There are various vehicles to access the utilities sector. In my view, exchange traded funds offer one of the best ways to capitalize on the broader market trends. Especially, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF offers a cheap way to access the best companies through a single investment.

XLU's portfolio offers greater exposure to the electric utility companies, making up 65% of XLU's portfolio weight. The independent power and renewable electricity producers represent 5%, while the multi-utilities weight in its portfolio is around 25%.

XLU's portfolio comprises 34 stocks, with the top 10 accounting for 59.6%. Its portfolio includes the top-rated companies, such as NextEra Energy, Constellation Energy, Southern Co (SO), Duke Energy Corp, American Electric Power (AEP), Dominion Energy (D), Exelon (EXC) and many others. The fund's estimated earnings growth in the next 3 to 5 years is around 8.20%.

XLU's share price momentum appears strong, with a price surge of 22% so far in 2025. In addition, XLU also offers a high dividend yield of 2.5%. As the majority of companies are poised to deliver robust earnings and cash flow growth in the years ahead, the prospects of the healthy dividend hikes are also high.

Its expense ratio of 0.08% appears low compared to the median of all ETFs of 0.50%. The liquidity factor is also very strong with the $22 billion in assets under management and 11 million in average daily shares trading volume.

XLU earned a buy rating with a quant score of 3.95, according to the Seeking Alpha quant system. Its expense, liquidity, and dividend factors received an A plus grade, while a momentum factor gained a negative A grade. XLU is ranked second in its sub asset class. Moreover, XLU also appears cheap compared to the S&P 500, given its forward price to earnings ratio of 21. The fund's price to book ratio is around 2.50.

Risk Factors to Consider

Technically, XLU's standard deviation and annualized price volatility of 12.8 and 16.7% is in-line with the median of all ETFs.

The sector's performance can be impacted by regulatory challenges. This is because the majority of companies seeks to expand their power generation through investments in new projects, acquisitions, and mergers. Moreover, a lower than expected data center demand can also influence its performance. The tight electric vehicle policies and economic conditions can also dent the sector's performance.

In Conclusion

As the utilities sector is in its early stage of blistering growth trend, it might be the right time to initiate a position. The sector's valuations are also currently below the broader market index.

I believe investment vehicles, such as The Utilities Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF, could be worthwhile investment options to consider. XLU offers a greater exposure to fast-growing electric utilities. Its expense ratio is low, price momentum is robust, dividend is strong, and liquidity is healthy.

data.title: ETHD: Don't Be Fooled By This Yield On Ether
data.description:
Details matter

How a machine works internally is what tells us what the output of the machine will be. Given that most financial instruments are indeed machines with their own internal working parts this is also true of such financial instruments. We need to grasp how those internal parts interact to gain a good idea of what the likely outcome is going to be.

This always does mean a certain amount of boring detail but there we are.

Proshares UltraShort Ether ETF (NYSEARCA:ETHD)

This is, obviously enough, an exchange traded fund. This means that it's open ended - we buy and our money goes into the fund, we sell and it comes out. This potentially could lead to a problem - if many sell, then the fund might have to dump positions in order to pay out. Such dumping - at firesale prices - then damages the value of the fund, spooking more selling, and so we get a cascade - just like a bank run. Whether this can in fact happen depends upon the liquidity of the underlying investments being made by the fund itself. Here they're in ether futures and derivatives - these are liquid enough that it's not going to be a problem.

Another feature of an ETF is that it will always trade no more than tenths of a percent from net asset value.

The aim here is to provide 2x the negative of the ether price movement. That's not been a good long term bet this past year. But it's also true that this is a trading tool, not a hold, something I'll go into in more detail. Some days this was a very good bet - or perhaps we should say day trade or speculation.

For something to day trade, the spread looks a little wide, at 0.3%. But that's more a function of that spread being a penny on a $3 to $4 stock price.

We can trade in near $100,000 lots, so there's plenty of liquidity. Actual trade is in the 60 million pieces a day range currently.

Don't be fooled by the reported yield

The real point I want to get across here - a product of that attention to the necessary details - is that we must not get fooled by the reported yield. For this looks very tempting indeed:

Yield (TTM) 242.88%

Hold for 5 months, and everything after that is house money. Which would be very exciting, yes, but that's not the way it works, of course. This is the cash dividend of the past 12 months compared to the current market price - the trailing yield. And if the market price has collapsed over the past 12 months then the sums get a little misleading. And, yes, the price has collapsed - down 93% in fact, over 12 months.

So, we're counting dividends that might have been useful or reasonable 8 and 10 months ago as against a market price down 90%. Which bumps up that trailing yield.

The actual dividends paid in 12 months are here. About $9.15 cents for the past year. At a price a year back of some $55, that's a nice enough yield - near 17%. But it's also not 242%, and it's also not really making up for the 92% fall in capital value.

To gain an idea - and it will only be an idea because the future is uncertain - of the forward yield, what we think we might get, we can just annualize the most recent payout. Which was 0.004 cents, x 12 gives us a yield of 1.4% on a $3.50 stock. No, you're right, that's not correct, but that is indicative.

So, attention to detail. Where - entirely correctly - the trailing yield is listed as 240%, that's actually nothing, at all, to do with what we might be able to expect in the future.

The other way to put this is that this isn't a dividend stock. It wasn't in the past and it's most, most, unlikely to be in the future.

The internals at ETHD

The aim is to produce 2x the bear performance of ether. This is, of course, achieved by holding derivatives:

The expense ratio is 0.99%, which for an ETF dealing in options, etc positions is reasonable enough - fairly middle of the road. But we must always remember that fees aren't the point, it's returns after fees that are. Low fees for bad returns aren't worth paying and high fees for outperformance eminently so.

The aim here?

This ProShares ETF seeks daily investment results that correspond, before fees and expenses, to -2x the daily performance of its underlying benchmark (the “Daily Target”).

All well and good but note that "daily" there. This is a short term speculative tool, not a long term investment hold. This is so much so that Seeking Alpha gives a warning about it:

For example, consider the example of a 3x leveraged ETF where the benchmark rises from 100 to 102 (+2%) on day-1, and falls from 102 back to 100 (-1.96%) on day-2. The benchmark index is back to its original level of 100, so has flat performance.

For the leveraged ETF, however, the return would be negative. To illustrate, after the first day of trading, an initial $100 investment on a 3x leveraged ETF would rise to $106.

$100 initial + (($100 x 2% index return) x 3) = $106

The next day the 3x leveraged ETF would drop 5.88% (3 x -1.96%), resulting in a value of only $99.77.

$106 - 5.88% = $99.77

Even though the benchmark index was flat, the leveraged ETF would have resulted in a loss of -0.23% over these 2 days.

$99.77 - $100 initial value = $-0.23

As does the SEC:

Leveraged and inverse ETFs typically are designed to achieve their stated performance objectives on a daily basis. Some investors might invest in these ETFs with the expectation that the ETFs may meet their stated daily performance objectives over the long term as well. Investors should be aware that performance of these ETFs over a period longer than one day can differ significantly from their stated daily performance objectives and may potentially expose investors to significant and sudden losses.

So, be warned.

As to why this is true, the costs of running a leveraged, or a bear, position include two things. One is the daily price reset. The other is that they're gaining these positions through derivatives - they have to be. But derivatives have a time value - a time value which, obviously, expires over time.

Derivatives - whether packaged into an ETF or other fund structure, or directly - are excellent ways of gaining leverage for the short term. The longer the period we try to use them, the more expensive they become. So, for longer speculative periods, do not use derivatives - nor funds themselves using derivatives.

The proof of the long term holding problem

If - and do note if! - these leveraged and bear ETFs were good long term holdings then the bull and bear versions would produce roughly opposite returns. Sure, there's be some loss from management fees but if the one was 90% down then the other, the inverse, should be 90% up. Or, at least, around and about.

So, are they?

Well, OK, the ether price has moved away from those trying to be bear. That's OK. But if we'd held the bull version over the same time - say as a pair - we should be roughly flat, right? Flat minus expenses SAY?

Ah, no, we're not, are we? If we'd held the pair over the year we'd be about 45% down. Which proves that the leveraged ETFs are not the way to hold long term positions.

Why I'm wrong

As I'm not saying do bet on ether, or don't (and you shouldn't believe me if I did. I'm the guy who said that bitcoin is over in Forbes back in 2013) then I can't be right or wrong on that idea. The price of ether is a perfectly sensible thing to speculate upon. But which way isn't something for me.

But that is where I could be wrong is that ether could fall in which case ETHD is a fine way to play that as a short term position.

My opinion

I'm fine with a portfolio containing speculative positions - would even insist that a properly balanced one should. That means that speculative - derivative and leverage based - positions on crypto are equally fine with me. They should be minimal parts of such a balanced portfolio, of course. The spice and flavour after a pension has been assured perhaps.

The use of leveraged bear ETFs like this one is to be taking a bear position intraday. That might be because we're long the underlying ETH. Or it might be just that we think there's going to be a price move - either a hedge or a naked speculation.

But this instrument - this form of instrument - just isn't how to take long term positions, either bull or bear in anything. Even if it does work - and in a couple of instruments that tactic has - greater return would have been achieved by constructing the same exposure in a different way.

Leveraged - bear - ETFs are short term trading tools and should be used that way.

And as up above, the real point of this piece is that we should not get fooled by trailing yields. We need to grasp the details of the internal of the instrument - that way we can see, again as above, that that trailing yield really is no guide at all to the forward yield.

You're absolutely correct, 243% is a very exciting yield. But that is the past 12 month cash distribution on a market price that has fallen 93%. The next 12 month distribution really isn't going to be like that at all.

data.title: Rubrik: The Cybersecurity Game-Changer That Investors Shouldn't Overlook
data.description:
Rubrik (RBRK) is a misunderstood company that may be difficult for some people to understand. The company's Co-founder and Chief Executive Officer ("CEO"), Bipul Sinha, said at the 2025 Goldman Sachs Communacopia + Technology Conference:

I'm happily dissatisfied because Rubrik will always be a misunderstood company because Rubrik will always be at the frontier of innovation. And I used to be an engineer and then I became a venture capitalist and now Founder, CEO. And I always believe that the best companies are counter consensus and misunderstood. That's when the value is created and that's when value is realized.

A significant reason people misunderstand the company is that they fail to grasp what the company does and its place in the competitive marketplace. When the company first entered the marketplace, it had disruptive data protection technology. Some investors still view it as a "backup and data recovery" platform. However, the company's CEO and management see themselves differently. Rubrik views itself as a cybersecurity company whose main product, Rubrik Security Cloud (RSC), is the last line of defense against modern threats like ransomware.

The company built this cloud product on a Zero Trust architecture ("ZTA"), which is a perimeterless cybersecurity framework that operates on the principle of never trusting, but constantly verifying every user, service, and device before granting limited access.

The company's website discusses ZTA:

To be effective, ZTA never assumes implicit trust based on a user's physical or network location. For example, just because a user is logged onto the Local Area Network (LAN), that should not entitle him or her to see any specific data available on the LAN. Nor does asset "ownership" matter. If user X is listed as the owner of a database, user X still needs to establish who they are before being allowed to access their database. ZT solutions use tools like Multi-Factor Authentication ("MFA") to authenticate users and then authorize discrete access permissions.

The company operates in multiple areas that integrate data security, risk analysis, and identity protection for use with backup data. This convergence space doesn't fit neatly into existing cybersecurity or IT infrastructure categories.

CEO Sinha believes his company has created a new cybersecurity category: cyber resilience, which is the ability of an organization to withstand and recover from cyber incidents. This ability is increasingly important in the age of artificial intelligence ("AI"). Although cybersecurity companies use AI to enhance their customers' security, this technology also empowers adversaries to create more sophisticated attacks, such as deepfakes and adaptive malware.

The company showed in its second quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2026 results that the market accepts Rubrik's "cyber resilience" value proposition through several key growth metrics.

Rubrik's subscription ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue) increased 36% year-over-year to $1.25 billion. This type of growth at scale indicates broad market adoption of its subscription-based platform, the RSC.

Subscription NRR (Net Revenue Retention) remained above 120%, indicating that the company's existing customers increased their recurring revenue by 20% over the previous year. Existing customers are not only staying but also significantly increasing their spending with Rubrik.

The company's Chief Financial Officer ("CFO"), Kiran Choudary, noted on the second quarter FY 2026 earnings call that "Adoption of additional security functionality contributed approximately 35% of our subscription net retention rate in the quarter," clear financial evidence that customers are interested in the cyber resilience concept by voting with their wallets. Customers are buying the added security layers, not just the basic backup product.

Customers with $100,000 or greater in subscription ARR increased 27% year-over-year to 2,505 customers. These larger customers increased from 82% of the total subscription ARR last year to 85% this year, indicating Rubrik is becoming a strategic partner to large enterprises, which aligns with the high-stakes, board-level issue of cyber resilience.

CFO Choudary said the following on the second-quarter earnings call:

We remain confident about our outlook given the strength of the cyber resilience market and demand for our differentiated offerings. We believe these drivers alongside our strong and consistent execution will deliver strong subscription ARR growth ahead.

As long as the market believes the company can achieve robust revenue growth, investors will award it a premium valuation, primarily because the market values the stock based on subscription ARR growth and revenue growth.

Rubrik Customer Wins

CEO Sinha detailed a customer win on the second-quarter earnings call that illustrated why the idea of cyber resilience and an "assume a breach" mindset is becoming popular in the C-suites of large companies:

This unique architecture consistently enables us to outperform both legacy and new-gen backup vendors. Let me highlight this with 2 illustrative customer wins from the quarter. A major North American oil and gas company selected Rubrik after its legacy backup provider was unable to support a fast recovery following a disruptive cyber attack. Rubrik was selected because of our superior recovery time relative to both legacy as well as new-gen alternatives. Our comprehensive yet radically simple platform for cyber recovery across all workloads, including the cloud, was another key reason for the legacy backup replacement.

The above customer win is an example of Rubrik replacing a legacy backup provider that was unable to satisfy the customer's needs. The major North American oil and gas company chose Rubrik because of its superior performance over rivals, validating Rubrik management's core message that traditional backup solutions are insufficient for modern cyber threats.

The CEO also highlighted a significant win with a Fortune 50 pharma leader:

A Fortune 50 pharma leader turned to Rubrik to protect its critical applications, displacing its 20-year-old legacy backup vendor as well as native cloud backup solutions. We also outcompeted new-gen backup vendors for this opportunity. Rubrik was selected due to not only our ability to deliver greater cyber resiliency in the face of escalating cyber risk, but also more efficient cloud storage costs.

The above customer win exemplifies Rubrik replacing a 20-year-old legacy vendor and native cloud backup solutions due to its ability to provide better cyber resiliency against rising cyber risk.

Rubrik also won in areas outside its original backup and data recovery business. The company now has an identity resilience business that it rolled out in April 2025. CEO Sinha said on the earnings call, "We are the only vendor in the market that delivers rapid recovery of both Active Directory and Entra ID in a hybrid cloud manner, the backbone of identity solutions worldwide."

Active Directory is Microsoft's on-premises directory service for managing user identities and network resources, and Entra ID is Microsoft's cloud-based ID service. The CEO highlighted two wins in identity resilience: a U.K. financial services company and a Fortune 500 financial institution. Both companies found issues with their Active Directory environment. CEO Sinha said specifically about the Fortune 500 win:

A Fortune 500 financial institution in the U.S. turned to Rubrik after an audit uncovered that its Active Directory recovery could take upwards of 7 days, with millions of dollars at risk each day. By adding Rubrik Identity Recovery, they reduced recovery times to under 2 hours, preventing potentially significant business disruption and satisfying Board mandates.

The company likely considered it essential to highlight Identity wins, as Data & Identity Survivability is a relatively new service that follows the establishment of its foundational Backup and Recovery service. The CEO wants investors to understand that Rubrik is not just a one-trick pony.

All of the above wins involve large enterprises, which is where investors want to see the wins. Large enterprise deals typically result in predictable, subscription-based ARR that is less likely to churn than smaller companies, which helps the company produce a solid NRR. Investors will often award a company with an NRR above 120% a premium valuation.

Additionally, when Rubrik serves enterprise customers, it drives economies of scale, which improves gross margins (nearly 80%). Economies of scale mean that as revenue grows, profitability increases at an even faster rate, helping the company move towards breaking even and eventually becoming profitable.

Generally, investors will award companies with increasing gross margins through economies of scale with a premium valuation.

Lastly, large enterprise wins in a highly competitive market signal that Rubrik is gaining market share against entrenched legacy vendors with its modern cloud-native platform, proving its superiority against older technology.

Valuation

Rubrik's trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 13.69, well above the Information Technology ("IT") median of 3.53, a sign of potential overvaluation.

The following chart compares Rubrik's TTM P/S ratio to several partial or direct competitors. Commvault Systems (CVLT) is the most similar and is a direct competitor to Rubrik in the comparison.

Although Rubrik achieved the highest quarterly year-over-year revenue growth rate of its peers, Broadcom (AVGO), Datadog (DDOG), and Zscaler (ZS) have higher P/S ratios. If investors only compare revenue growth rates, some might consider Rubrik undervalued compared to Broadcom, Datadog, and Zscaler. Some may interpret Rubrik's faster revenue growth rate over Commvault Systems (CVLT), Okta (OKTA), and NetApp (NTAP) as justifying its higher TTM P/S ratio and may not consider Rubrik overvalued compared to those companies.

The following table compares Rubrik's one-year forward P/S to the estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) ratio and trailing 12-month ("TTM") profitability figures. Generally, the market considers the company with the lowest P/SG ratio as undervalued compared to companies with higher P/S/G ratios. Given Rubrik's lowest P/S/G in the comparison, some may consider it undervalued.

Company Name One-year Forward P/S ratio One Year Forward estimated revenue growth One-year forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth TTM FCF margin TTM Gross Margin TTM Operating Margin TTM ROIC
Rubrik 13.31 39.23% 0.34 16.66% 77.96% -39.58% -----
Commvault Systems 6.95 16.87% 0.41 17.83% 81.02% 8.59% 25.12%
Broadcom 26.60 22.79% 1.16 41.59% 63.13% 39.33% 13.77%
Zscaler 15.13 22.53% 0.67 27.18% 76.82% -4.48% -1.42%
Datadog 17.20 24.03% 0.72 28.32% 79.90% -0.61% 3.00%
Okta 5.51 10.50% 0.52 30.36% 76.91% 3.00% 2.26%
NetApp 3.51 2.95% 1.19 25.15% 69.76% 21.80% 32.14%

The high quality of Rubrik's business (relatively high gross margin of 77.96%), combined with its aggressive growth (one-year forward estimated revenue growth of 39.23%, the highest in the group), makes its low P/S/G ratio of 0.34 intriguing for growth investors. The company is generating solid Free Cash Flow ("FCF") despite significant operating losses, validating its high growth strategy and suggesting that slowing research and development (R&D) spend could quickly lead to high profitability.

Rubrik's forward FY 2029 price-to-earnings (P/E) to estimated earnings-per-share ("EPS") growth ("PEG") ratio is 0.60. Generally, the market considers a PEG ratio below 1.0 as a sign of undervaluation. Suppose the company traded at an FY 2029 PEG ratio of 1.0; the stock price would be $129, a 39.41% decline from its October 14 price of $78.17.

The company's forward fiscal year ("FY") 2030 PEG ratio is 1.12, which some might interpret as a fair valuation. However, the problem with using a forward P/E ratio with this company is that analysts expect EPS to turn profitable several years from now. The further out that analysts attempt to estimate EPS, the more inaccurate the estimate, which is likely why only one analyst tried to estimate out to FY2029 and FY2030. So, take this PEG ratio analysis with a massive grain of salt.

Some might value the company using its FCF-based valuations. However, Rubrik is in the hyper-growth stage, which can make FCF-based valuations highly inaccurate. The best valuation method for Rubrik today is revenue-based valuations because the stock is speculative. Investors in the company speculate that it can sustain high revenue growth rates while eventually achieving profitability. If revenue growth slows for any reason, current investors may see downside.

Risks

Although most of the ways that I have chosen to value the stock suggest undervaluation, on an absolute basis, the stock sells at a premium P/S valuation to the IT sector and the market, suggesting that market expectations are already high. If the company's revenue forecasts disappoint, the stock could sell off.
